summary: Map tt-metal stock data_movement tests in the local clone to author an all-core 3-hop gather/scatter kernel

maps.component: all_to_all data_movement test (single-device, L1->L1, all master cores unicast-write to all subordinate cores). Host: tests/tt_metal/tt_metal/data_movement/all_to_all/test_all_to_all.cpp. Kernels: kernels/sender.cpp (gen1 dataflow API) and kernels/sender_2_0.cpp (Metal 2.0 Noc/UnicastEndpoint API, the one actually wired into the ProgramSpec). Helpers: dm_common.{hpp,cpp}. BW post-processing: python/stats_collector.py. NOTE for the requested 3-hop dual-NoC gather/scatter kernel: this existing test is a useful template but is SINGLE-HOP, SINGLE-NOC-per-run UNICAST only. It does NOT do multi-hop routing, does NOT run NOC0 and NOC1 simultaneously (noc chosen wholesale per run via test_config.noc_id), and has no X-then-Y/Y-then-X partner-direction logic. You will have to add: (a) two kernels or one kernel split across RISCV_0/RISCV_1 to drive NOC0+NOC1 concurrently, (b) per-core partner/hop computation, (c) directional partner selection. The reusable pieces are: all-core CoreRange selection, worker_core_from_logical_core coordinate packing into varargs, the noc_async_write+barrier loop, and the profiler DeviceTimestampedData -> stats_collector aggregate-bandwidth pipeline.
maps.host_setup: Device: single device pulled from a MeshDevice via mesh_device->impl().get_device(0) (test_all_to_all.cpp:59); the suite is GenericMeshDeviceFixture running a single-device unit mesh, fast-dispatch only (README:5-8). Program is launched as a MeshWorkload at MeshCoordinate(0,0) (test:199-206), then Finish(cq). CoreRange: master grid = CoreRange(mst_logical_start_coord, mst_logical_end_coord) where end = start + grid_size - 1 (test:76-81); subordinate grid built identically (test:85-90); CoreRangeSet wraps the single CoreRange. For the all-to-all directed-ideal/packet-sizes cases the grid is the FULL compute grid: mst_grid_size = sub_grid_size = {device->compute_with_storage_grid_size().x, .y} (test:446-447, 342-343, 400-401). num_subordinates = sub_logical_core_set.num_cores() (test:91). CB: none used; this test writes directly to raw L1 (no CircularBuffer). L1 addressing via HAL: get_l1_address_and_size() returns hal.get_dev_addr/get_dev_size(core_type, HalL1MemAddrType::DEFAULT_UNRESERVED) (dm_common.cpp:30-35); mst_l1_base_address = that base, sub_l1_base_address = mst base + bytes_per_transaction (test:116-118). Semaphores: NONE (pure async write + write barrier, no handshake). Program build uses the Metal 2.0 experimental host API: KernelSpec with compile_time_args bound by name (test_id, mst_l1_addr, sub_l1_addr, num_subordinates, num_vc) at test:124-149, .hw_config = DataMovementHardwareConfig{ gen1_config{ .processor = DataMovementProcessor::RISCV_0, .noc = test_config.noc_id } } so ONE RISCV processor on ONE NOC per run; .advanced_options.num_runtime_varargs = num_subordinates*2 (test:131,148). ProgramSpec has one WorkUnitSpec with target_nodes = mst_logical_core_set (test:151-159), built via MakeProgramFromSpec. Runtime args set per master core through ProgramRunArgs/SetProgramRunArgs (test:163-174). Profiler init is implicit via DeviceZoneScopedN/DeviceTimestampedData in the kernel.
maps.kernel_code: sender_2_0.cpp (Metal 2.0, the active kernel): reads named compile-time args via get_arg(args::...) (test_id, mst_l1_addr, sub_l1_addr, num_subordinates, num_vc) and runtime args num_of_transactions, bytes_per_transaction; reads the subordinate (x,y) pairs from varargs into std::array<std::array<uint32_t,2>,num_subordinates> via get_vararg (sender_2_0.cpp:23-28). Creates Noc noc(noc_index) and UnicastEndpoint. Inside DeviceZoneScopedN(\"RISCV0\"): outer loop j over num_subordinates, inner loop i over num_of_transactions issuing noc.async_write<NocOptions::CUSTOM_VC>(src endpoint addr=master_l1_local_address, dst endpoint {noc_x, noc_y, addr=subordinate_l1_local_address}, size=bytes_per_transaction, vc = i % num_virtual_channels) (sender_2_0.cpp:42-58). After both loops a single noc.async_write_barrier() (sender_2_0.cpp:60) — barrier is OUTSIDE both loops, once per kernel, so all writes to all subordinates are in flight concurrently before the one barrier. sender.cpp (gen1 equivalent): identical structure using noc_async_write(master_l1_local_address, subordinate_l1_noc_address, bytes, noc_index, current_virtual_channel) (sender.cpp:43-49) with subordinate_l1_noc_address = get_noc_addr(x,y,addr) computed once per subordinate (sender.cpp:37-38), single noc_async_write_barrier() after the loops (sender.cpp:51). There is NO multicast, NO multi-hop, NO NOC0+NOC1 concurrency in either kernel — it is plain unicast async_write fan-out. master address is fixed (same source buffer reused to every subordinate); subordinate dst address is fixed sub_l1_base_address on every core.
maps.partner_coord_logic: Host computes physical NoC coords up front: for each logical subordinate core in corerange_to_cores(sub_logical_core_set), call device->worker_core_from_logical_core(sub_logical_core) to get the physical worker (NoC) CoreCoord, then push x then y into a flat vector<uint32_t> sub_worker_coordinates (test:93-100). That flat [x0,y0,x1,y1,...] vector is passed identically to EVERY master core as runtime varargs: sender_run_params.advanced_options.runtime_varargs.emplace(mst_logical_core, sub_worker_coordinates) (test:171). So every master core's partner set = ALL subordinate cores (literal all-to-all); there is no per-core/per-hop partner selection or directional (east/south vs north/west) logic in the existing test. On device, the kernel forms the remote NoC address from those physical coords: gen1 uses get_noc_addr(subordinate_x_coord, subordinate_y_coord, subordinate_l1_local_address) (sender.cpp:37-38); gen2 passes {.noc_x, .noc_y, .addr} directly into the UnicastEndpoint dst (sender_2_0.cpp:52-56). NOTE for your 3-hop dual-NoC kernel: you must add the logical->physical mapping for the chosen hop partner (still via worker_core_from_logical_core on host, or compute it on device) and add the NOC0=X-then-Y(east/south)/NOC1=Y-then-X(north/west) directional partner derivation, which does not exist here.
maps.size_iter_params: bytes_per_transaction = pages_reservable_per_transaction * bytes_per_page (test:112). bytes_per_page from arch via compute_physical_constraints -> obtain_page_size_bytes: 64B on BLACKHOLE, 32B otherwise (dm_common.cpp:21, 47-58). Directed-ideal sizing: num_of_transactions_per_master = 1 and pages_reservable_per_transaction = max_reservable_pages / num_of_transactions / 2 (half L1 for sender half for receiver) (test:246-248); max_reservable_pages = DEFAULT_UNRESERVED L1 size / bytes_per_page (dm_common.cpp:54-56). Packet-sizes sweep: num_of_transactions 1..256 (*=4) x pages 1..(BH 1024 / WH 2048) (*=2), capped to 1/1 on QUASAR (test:287-330). Virtual-channels sweep loops NOC_0 and NOC_1, pages 1..4096, vc 1..4, num_of_transactions fixed 256 (test:345-382). Passing convention: test_id, mst_l1_addr, sub_l1_addr, num_subordinates, num_vc are COMPILE-TIME args (KernelSpec.compile_time_args, test:124-129); num_of_transactions and bytes_per_transaction are RUNTIME args (runtime_arg_schema + ProgramRunArgs, test:138, 169-170); the per-subordinate (x,y) physical coords are RUNTIME VARARGS (num_runtime_varargs = num_subordinates*2, test:131,148,171). bytes_per_transaction_per_master is the per-write size; total bytes moved per master = bytes_per_transaction * num_of_transactions * num_subordinates.
maps.bw_measurement: Measured on-device by the profiler, computed offline in Python. Kernel wraps the whole write+barrier region in DeviceZoneScopedN(\"RISCV0\") (sender_2_0.cpp:37 / sender.cpp:28) giving start/end RISC cycle timestamps (duration_cycles), and stamps metadata via DeviceTimestampedData: \"Test id\", \"NoC Index\", \"Number of transactions\" (= num_of_transactions * num_subordinates), \"Transaction size in bytes\", \"Number of Virtual Channels\" (sender_2_0.cpp:63-67; sender.cpp:54-59 also adds \"Number of subordinates\"). Post-processing in python/stats_collector.py groups profiler series by run_host_id per RISC (stats_collector.py:152-187): per-core effective bandwidth = num_transactions * transaction_size / duration_cycles (line 169-170); aggregate per run = median (or mean) across cores of duration and bandwidth (lines 199-203). AGGREGATE/COMBINED NoC bandwidth (the all-core number you want): wall_clock_time = max(end_cycle over cores) - min(start_cycle over cores) (line 211-213); total_bytes = num_cores * transaction_size * num_transactions unless every core stamped \"Per-core bytes\" in which case it sums those (lines 219-224); combined_bandwidth = total_bytes / wall_clock_time (line 225); and bandwidth_gbps = agg_bandwidth * clock_freq_ghz when \"Clock frequency MHz\" is stamped (lines 228-230). These land in agg_data fields combined_bandwidth, total_bytes, wall_clock_time, num_cores, bandwidth_gbps (lines 232-251). The C++ run_dm itself only does a correctness equality check (reads each subordinate's sub_l1 region and compares to the golden input, test:214-229); it does NOT compute bandwidth — bandwidth comes entirely from the profiler+Python pipeline keyed by runtime_host_id (test:177-178, dm_common.cpp:18).
gotchas: This test is SINGLE-NOC PER RUN, not dual-NoC concurrent: the NOC is fixed by DataMovementHardwareConfig.gen1_config.noc = test_config.noc_id and the kernel uses the implicit noc_index. To drive NOC0 AND NOC1 at once you must launch two kernels (or use both RISCV_0 and RISCV_1 data-movement processors), one bound to each NOC; nothing here does that.

This test is SINGLE-HOP unicast fan-out, no multi-hop/3-hop routing and no multicast. The 'all-to-all' is achieved by each master directly unicasting to every subordinate. Your 3-hop gather/scatter routing/partner math must be added from scratch.

No directional (east/south vs north/west, X-then-Y vs Y-then-X) partner logic exists. NoC dimension-order routing is a hardware property; the test does not select partners by direction. I did not find host/kernel code in this test encoding X-first/Y-first partner choice.

The single noc_async_write_barrier() is OUTSIDE both loops (one per kernel), so all transactions to all partners overlap. If you need per-hop ordering for a 3-hop pipeline you must insert intermediate barriers/semaphores (none exist here).

Two kernel variants exist: sender.cpp (gen1 dataflow_api) and sender_2_0.cpp (Metal 2.0 Noc/UnicastEndpoint). The ProgramSpec wires ONLY sender_2_0.cpp (test:135). sender.cpp is legacy/reference and also stamps an extra 'Number of subordinates'.

Aggregate bandwidth is NOT computed in C++ run_dm (that only does an equality correctness check). It is derived offline by python/stats_collector.py from profiler timestamps; combined_bandwidth = total_bytes/wall_clock_time and total_bytes defaults to num_cores*transaction_size*num_transactions unless each core stamps 'Per-core bytes'. For a gather/scatter where per-core byte counts differ, you MUST stamp 'Per-core bytes' per core or the uniform formula will mis-count aggregate bandwidth.

L1 layout reserves master region [mst_l1_base, mst_l1_base+bytes_per_transaction) and subordinate region immediately after; directed-ideal halves max pages between sender/receiver (test:248). A 3-hop kernel needs its own non-overlapping intermediate/relay buffer carve-out beyond this two-region split.
Page size differs by arch: 64B on Blackhole vs 32B elsewhere (dm_common.cpp:21); packet-size sweep cap is 1024 pages on Blackhole vs 2048 (test:288-290). Honor 64B flit alignment on Blackhole.
num_virtual_channels must be <= 4 (only 4 unicast write VCs); host returns false otherwise (test:103-108).

Tests run via GenericMeshDeviceFixture on a single-device unit mesh, fast-dispatch only (no slow dispatch); program launched as a MeshWorkload at MeshCoordinate(0,0). The all-core full-grid sizing comes from device->compute_with_storage_grid_size().

maps.component: all_from_all data_movement test (gather / NOC async-read direction). Each "master" core reads from all "subordinate" cores into its own L1. Files live under /home/starboy/tt-metal/tests/tt_metal/tt_metal/data_movement/all_from_all/. Host driver: test_all_from_all.cpp; gather kernels: kernels/requestor.cpp (Gen1 legacy NOC API) and kernels/requestor_2_0.cpp (Metal 2.0 Noc/UnicastEndpoint API). This is the read/gather analog of all_to_all's scatter/write.
files: /home/starboy/tt-metal/tests/tt_metal/tt_metal/data_movement/all_from_all/README.md:18-27 (test flow: master issues NOC reads from every subordinate, equality check)
maps.host_setup: run_dm() (test_all_from_all.cpp:59). Device obtained via mesh_device->impl().get_device(0). Two CoreRangeSets: master ("mst") at line 79-84 and subordinate ("sub") at 88-93, each built as CoreRange(start, start+grid_size-1). num_subordinates = sub set num_cores() (94). Subordinate PHYSICAL coords are precomputed host-side: for each logical sub core, device->worker_core_from_logical_core(...) gives the worker (physical) CoreCoord, and x,y are pushed flat into sub_worker_coordinates (96-103). L1 addressing: get_l1_address_and_size(...DEFAULT_UNRESERVED) returns base; sub_l1_base_address = base, mst_l1_base_address = base + bytes_per_transaction (110-112), so master read buffer sits right after the subordinate buffer in the same L1 map. This is Metal 2.0 host API: KernelSpec with CompileTimeArgs map {test_id, mst_l1_addr, sub_l1_addr, num_subordinates, num_vc} (118-123); hw_config = DataMovementHardwareConfig with Gen1Config{processor=RISCV_1, noc=test_config.noc_id} (133-141); advanced_options.num_runtime_varargs = num_subordinates*2 to carry the coord array (125,142). ProgramSpec has one WorkUnitSpec targeting mst_logical_core_set (148-153); Program = MakeProgramFromSpec. Per-master runtime args set via ProgramRunArgs/SetProgramRunArgs: scalar args {num_of_transactions, bytes_per_transaction} plus runtime_varargs = sub_worker_coordinates for every master core (157-168). Default noc_id = NOC::NOC_1 (struct line 46; directed_ideal/packet_sizes line 230,271); virtual_channels_test sweeps both NOC_0 and NOC_1 (line 345). No CircularBuffer and no Semaphore are created (pure L1<->L1 read, data pre-seeded by host). Launch: distributed::MeshWorkload + EnqueueMeshWorkload + Finish (191-199).
maps.kernel_code: Gather/read kernel runs on each MASTER core (RISCV_1 / NCRISC), reading FROM every subordinate INTO local L1. requestor.cpp (Gen1, kernel_main): CTAs = test_id, mst_l1_base, sub_l1_base, num_of_transactions, bytes_per_transaction, num_subordinates, num_vc. Outer loop j over num_subordinates: read this sub's physical x = get_arg_val<uint32_t>(j*2), y = get_arg_val<uint32_t>(j*2+1); build remote address subordinate_l1_noc_address = get_noc_addr(sub_x, sub_y, subordinate_l1_local_address) (line 37-38). Inner loop i over num_of_transactions: current_virtual_channel = i % num_virtual_channels; noc_async_read(subordinate_l1_noc_address, master_l1_local_address, bytes_per_transaction_per_subordinate, noc_index, current_virtual_channel) (43-48). After BOTH loops, a single noc_async_read_barrier() (51) drains all outstanding reads. Note: master_l1_local_address is NOT advanced between subordinates, so every read lands at the same master offset (the test only verifies the final/last subordinate's data against golden). The whole transfer is wrapped in DeviceZoneScopedN("RISCV1") (28) for cycle measurement. requestor_2_0.cpp is the Metal 2.0 equivalent: Noc noc(noc_index); UnicastEndpoint; same double loop calling noc.async_read<NocOptions::CUSTOM_VC>(src_endpoint, dst_endpoint, bytes, {.noc_x, .noc_y, .addr=sub_addr}, {.addr=mst_addr}, NocOptVals{.vc=current_virtual_channel}) (44-56), followed by noc.async_read_barrier() (59). Direction is READ/gather: noc_async_read pulls remote->local (opposite of all_to_all which uses noc_async_write to push local->remote).
maps.partner_coord_logic: Partner = each subordinate (responder) core that a master reads from. Logical->physical happens HOST-side, not in the kernel: for every subordinate logical CoreCoord, device->worker_core_from_logical_core(sub_logical_core) yields the physical/worker CoreCoord; its .x and .y are appended (flat, x then y) into sub_worker_coordinates (test:99-103). That flat array is delivered to every master as runtime varargs (test:165). In the kernel the master reads them back in pairs (requestor.cpp: get_arg_val(j*2), get_arg_val(j*2+1)) and forms the 64-bit NoC address with get_noc_addr(sub_x, sub_y, sub_l1_local_addr) (requestor.cpp:37-38) — i.e. the physical coords are already resolved, get_noc_addr just packs the NOC_XY encoding + local L1 offset. The Metal 2.0 kernel passes the same physical coords directly into the endpoint struct {.noc_x, .noc_y, .addr} (requestor_2_0.cpp:49-52). The physical x/y direction of travel (X-then-Y vs Y-then-X) is implied by which NoC executes the transaction: it is selected purely via the `noc`/`noc_index` argument (Gen1Config.noc = test_config.noc_id; default NOC_1, sweep covers NOC_0+NOC_1 in virtual_channels_test). The test does no manual NOC0-east/south vs NOC1-north/west partitioning — that directional split is something the new dual-NoC 3-hop kernel must add on top of this pattern.
maps.size_iter_params: Transfer size: bytes_per_transaction = pages_reservable_per_transaction * bytes_per_page (test:106). bytes_per_page from arch: 64B Blackhole, 32B Wormhole (dm_common.cpp:21). Iteration count: num_of_transactions_per_subordinate (per remote core); total reads issued per master = num_of_transactions * num_subordinates. Arg passing split: CompileTime (constexpr, via KernelSpec.compile_time_args map) = test_id, mst_l1_addr, sub_l1_addr, num_subordinates, num_vc (test:118-123). Runtime scalar args = num_of_transactions, bytes_per_transaction (test:132,162-164). Runtime VARARGS = the flat subordinate physical-coord array, length num_subordinates*2 (test:125,142,165). Sweep bounds in packet_sizes_test: num_of_transactions 1..256 (*=4), pages_per_transaction 1..(1024 BH / 2048 WH) (*=2), capped at max_reservable_pages so total transaction <= 64KB (test:278-294). directed_ideal_test: 1 transaction, pages = max_reservable_pages/2 (half L1 for master read buffer, half for subordinate source) (test:237-239). Page size constraint: compute_physical_constraints returns {bytes_per_page, max_reservable_bytes, max_reservable_pages} from DEFAULT_UNRESERVED L1 size / page_size (dm_common.cpp:47-59).
maps.bw_measurement: Device-side: the read+barrier region is wrapped in DeviceZoneScopedN("RISCV1") (requestor.cpp:28, requestor_2_0.cpp:36) which records start/end device cycles for that scope. Per-run profiler attributes are emitted with DeviceTimestampedData: "Test id", "Number of transactions" (= num_of_transactions * num_subordinates), "Transaction size in bytes", "NoC Index", "Number of Virtual Channels" (requestor.cpp:54-61). Host-side post-processing (python/stats_collector.py): per-core effective bandwidth = num_transactions * transaction_size / duration_cycles (line 168-170); aggregate = median across cores (line 200); combined/aggregate NoC bandwidth = total_bytes / wall_clock_time where total_bytes = num_cores * transaction_size * num_transactions and wall_clock_time = max_end_cycle - min_start_cycle across all cores (lines 207-225) — this is the metric for AGGREGATE all-core NoC bandwidth. Converted to GB/s by multiplying cycles/sec: bandwidth_gbps = agg_bandwidth * clock_freq_ghz from the device-logged "Clock frequency MHz" (lines 227-230). Bounds/regression checks live in python/test_mappings/test_bounds.yaml + performance_checker.py; test_information.yaml maps ids 310-320 to pattern "all_from_all".
key_apis: noc_async_read(src_noc_addr, dst_local_l1_addr, size, noc, read_req_vc) — dataflow_api.h:552
get_noc_addr(noc_x, noc_y, addr) — builds 64-bit remote NoC address from physical coords
DeviceZoneScopedN("RISCV1") / DeviceTimestampedData(...) — profiler scope + attribute stamps
SetProgramRunArgs / ProgramRunArgs.runtime_varargs — per-core runtime arg + vararg delivery
detail::WriteToDeviceL1 / detail::ReadFromDeviceL1 + l1_barrier — host L1 seed/verify
compute_physical_constraints / get_l1_address_and_size (dm_common) — page size + L1 budget
gotchas: NO SEMAPHORE / NO HANDSHAKE anywhere in all_from_all (grep confirmed empty). It is a pull/gather: the subordinate source data is pre-written by the HOST via detail::WriteToDeviceL1 + l1_barrier BEFORE the program launches (test:185-188), so the master can read immediately and only needs a single noc_async_read_barrier to know reads completed. A real all-core gather where producers compute data on-device WOULD need a producer->consumer semaphore handshake (noc_semaphore_set/wait/inc) — this test sidesteps that. The new 3-hop dual-NoC kernel will likely need that handshake added.
Only ONE noc_async_read_barrier after BOTH loops (requestor.cpp:51) — all reads across all subordinates and all transactions are in flight simultaneously; barrier drains the whole batch.

master_l1_local_address is never incremented across subordinates or transactions (requestor.cpp:20, no update in loop) — every read overwrites the same master offset, so only the LAST subordinate's data survives. The equality check (test:209) compares against a single golden = the original input, and host pre-seeds every subordinate with the SAME packed_input (test:177-186), so the check still passes. Aggregate-bandwidth measurement does NOT depend on distinct destinations.

Direction (NOC0 X-then-Y east/south vs NOC1 Y-then-X north/west) is selected ONLY by the noc/noc_index argument; this test does not split traffic by direction. Default is NOC_1; only virtual_channels_test (currently GTEST_SKIP'd, line 541) sweeps both NOCs. The dual-NoC directional split is new work, not present here.

Read-request VC: noc_async_read's read_req_vc defaults to NOC_UNICAST_WRITE_VC; read requests use a static VC while read RESPONSES get VCs assigned dynamically (dataflow_api.h:558-560) — so cycling i % num_virtual_channels only affects the request VC, not response routing.
Blackhole page size is 64B (vs 32B WH) and max pages/transaction capped at 1024 (BH) vs 2048 (WH) (test:279-281) to keep total transaction <= 64KB.

Kernel runs on RISCV_1 (NCRISC) only (DataMovementProcessor::RISCV_1, test:137); single num_threads=1. Aggregate dual-NoC throughput in this harness comes from running on MANY master cores concurrently, not from one core driving both NoCs.

Two API generations coexist: the active host code wires requestor_2_0.cpp (test:129); requestor.cpp (Gen1 legacy noc_async_read) is the comparison/reference. Citations for the literal noc_async_read+barrier+get_noc_addr pattern come from requestor.cpp.

maps.component: data_movement one_to_one (single sender->receiver L1->L1 write) and loopback (local L1->L1 self-write) reference tests, with shared dm_common helpers and the python profiler BW pipeline. These are the simplest references for a single NoC unicast write and a local L1->L1 copy, to base an all-core dual-NoC 3-hop gather/scatter kernel on.
maps.host_setup:
Both tests use the Metal 2.0 host API and run on a single-device unit mesh via GenericMeshDeviceFixture (fast dispatch only). Flow in run_dm():

DEVICE: `IDevice* device = mesh_device->impl().get_device(0)` (one_to_one:47, loopback:53).

CORE RANGES: logical CoreCoords wrapped in CoreRangeSet. one_to_one builds master_core_set and subordinate_core_set, then `combined_core_set = master_core_set.merge<CoreRangeSet>(subordinate_core_set)` (one_to_one:55-57). loopback uses the same CoreCoord for both master and subordinate sets (loopback:59-60).

L1 ADDRESS: `unit_tests::dm::get_l1_address_and_size(mesh_device, core)` returns {base_address,size} (dm_common.cpp:23). It maps logical->physical via `device->worker_core_from_logical_core(core)`, gets the programmable core type, then `hal.get_dev_addr(core_type, HalL1MemAddrType::DEFAULT_UNRESERVED)` / `hal.get_dev_size(...)` (dm_common.cpp:28-33). one_to_one uses the same base addr on both cores (src==dst addr); loopback puts dst at `base + transaction_size_bytes` so source and dest don't overlap on the same core (loopback:72-74), and requires `master_l1_info.size >= transaction_size_bytes * 2`.

KERNEL SPEC (experimental::KernelSpec): one_to_one passes compile-time args as a named map (KernelSpec::CompileTimeArgs cta_bindings_map) {l1_addr, test_id, dest_coords (packed), num_vc} (one_to_one:93-101), runtime arg schema names {num_tx, tx_size} (one_to_one:108), and hw_config = DataMovementHardwareConfig{ .gen1_config = { .processor = DataMovementProcessor::RISCV_0, .noc = test_config.noc_id }, .gen2_config = {} } (one_to_one:109-117). loopback's cta_bindings = {src_addr, dst_addr, page_size, test_id} (loopback:80-84), adds a SemaphoreSpec (SemaphoreSpecName \"sender_sem\", target = master core, loopback:86-89) bound into the kernel via KernelSpec::SemaphoreBinding{semaphore_spec_name, accessor_name=\"sem_name\"} (loopback:95-96), runtime arg schema {num_of_transactions, transaction_num_pages, dest_x, dest_y} (loopback:100), same RISCV_0 hw_config.

PROGRAM: `ProgramSpec{ .name, .kernels={sender_spec}, (.semaphores for loopback), .work_units = {WorkUnitSpec{ .kernels={sender_spec.unique_id}, .target_nodes = master_core_set }} }` then `Program program = MakeProgramFromSpec(*mesh_device, spec)` (one_to_one:120-130, loopback:113-124).

RUNTIME ARGS: ProgramRunArgs / ProgramRunArgs::KernelRunArgs; push {.node = master_core_coord, .args = {named runtime values}} then `SetProgramRunArgs(program, run_params)` (one_to_one:132-139, loopback:126-136). loopback passes dest_x/dest_y = worker.x/worker.y of its own physical core.

DATA + LAUNCH: write input with `detail::WriteToDeviceL1(device, core, addr, packed_input)` + `MetalContext::instance().get_cluster().l1_barrier(device->id())`; build a distributed::MeshWorkload, `mesh_workload.add_program(MeshCoordinateRange(MeshCoordinate({0,0})), std::move(program))`, then `auto& cq = mesh_device->mesh_command_queue(); distributed::EnqueueMeshWorkload(cq, mesh_workload, false); Finish(cq);` (one_to_one:161-170, loopback:158-165). Read back with `detail::ReadFromDeviceL1(...)` and compare vs golden (one_to_one:172-178, loopback:167-173). Each run gets a unique id via `program.set_runtime_id(unit_tests::dm::runtime_host_id++)`.
maps.kernel_code:
Both senders run a single RISCV_0 dataflow thread doing async writes inside a profiler zone, then a barrier.

one_to_one Gen1 sender.cpp: unpacks dest coords, `uint64_t dst_noc_addr = get_noc_addr(receiver_x_coord, receiver_y_coord, l1_local_addr)` (sender.cpp:22), then inside `{ DeviceZoneScopedN(\"RISCV0\"); for i in 0..num_of_transactions: vc = i % num_virtual_channels; noc_async_write(l1_local_addr, dst_noc_addr, bytes_per_transaction, noc_index, current_virtual_channel); }` followed by `noc_async_write_barrier();` (sender.cpp:24-31). Note it loops num_of_transactions writes all to the SAME dst (it is a bandwidth/throughput loop, not distinct addresses). `noc_index` is the implicit NoC selected by hw_config.

one_to_one Gen2 sender_2_0.cpp: `Noc noc(noc_index); UnicastEndpoint unicast_endpoint;` then `noc.async_write<NocOptions::CUSTOM_VC>(src_ep, dst_ep, bytes, {.addr=l1_local_addr}, {.noc_x=receiver_x_coord, .noc_y=receiver_y_coord, .addr=l1_local_addr}, NocOptVals{.vc=current_virtual_channel})` per iter, then `noc.async_write_barrier();` (sender_2_0.cpp:27-49).

loopback Gen1 sender.cpp: dst is on the SAME core (dest_x/dest_y = own physical coords), different L1 offset: `uint64_t dst_noc_addr = get_noc_addr(dest_x, dest_y, dst_addr); for i: noc_async_write(src_addr, dst_noc_addr, transaction_size_bytes); noc_async_write_barrier();` then signals completion via `noc_semaphore_inc(get_noc_addr(dest_x,dest_y,semaphore), 1)` (loopback sender.cpp:29-37).

loopback Gen2 sender_2_0.cpp: `Noc noc(noc_index); UnicastEndpoint unicast_endpoint; for i: noc.async_write(unicast_endpoint, unicast_endpoint, transaction_size_bytes, {.addr=src_addr}, {.noc_x=dest_x,.noc_y=dest_y,.addr=dst_addr}); noc.async_write_barrier();` then `Semaphore sem(sem::sem_name); sem.up(noc, dest_x, dest_y, 1);` (loopback sender_2_0.cpp:29-47).

all_to_all sender.cpp (multi-target reference relevant to a gather/scatter all-core kernel): loops over num_subordinates, reads each subordinate's (x,y) from runtime args `subordinate_x_coord = get_arg_val<uint32_t>(j*2); subordinate_y_coord = get_arg_val<uint32_t>(j*2+1);`, computes `get_noc_addr(x,y,sub_addr)`, then inner loop of num_of_transactions noc_async_writes; single `noc_async_write_barrier()` after all targets (all_to_all sender.cpp:29-52). No multicast or DYNAMIC_NOC pattern exists in these reference tests — NoC direction (X-then-Y vs Y-then-X) is implicit per NoC index, chosen on the host via hw_config.noc.
maps.partner_coord_logic:
Logical->physical conversion is done on the HOST and the physical coords are passed into the kernel.

one_to_one: `CoreCoord physical_subordinate_core = device->worker_core_from_logical_core(test_config.subordinate_core_coord)` then packs into one CTA: `packed_subordinate_core_coordinates = physical_subordinate_core.x << 16 | (physical_subordinate_core.y & 0xFFFF)` (one_to_one:87-89). Kernel unpacks: `receiver_x = packed >> 16; receiver_y = packed & 0xFFFF` and forms the remote address with `get_noc_addr(receiver_x, receiver_y, l1_local_addr)` (one_to_one sender.cpp:19-22). For the directed-ideal/perf case the partner is an ADJACENT core (master {0,0}, subordinate {0,1}) to minimize hops (one_to_one:402-403, README ID 50).

loopback: partner == self. `CoreCoord worker = device->worker_core_from_logical_core(test_config.master_core_coord)` (loopback:76); worker.x/worker.y passed as dest_x/dest_y runtime args (loopback:133-134); kernel uses `get_noc_addr(dest_x, dest_y, dst_addr)` to its own physical core.

all_to_all: each subordinate physical coord pushed as a pair of runtime args; kernel reads them at index j*2 / j*2+1.

KEY APIs for partner/NoC: host `worker_core_from_logical_core` (logical->physical/worker grid), kernel `get_noc_addr(x, y, local_addr)` (builds 64-bit NoC address from physical x,y + local L1 addr). The NoC used (NOC_0 vs NOC_1, i.e. which routing direction) is selected entirely on the host through `DataMovementHardwareConfig::Gen1Config{.noc = test_config.noc_id}` and surfaces in-kernel as `noc_index`; the kernel does NOT pick direction. NOC::NOC_0 / NOC::NOC_1 are swept in one_to_one's virtual_channels_test loop `for (NOC noc_id : {NOC::NOC_0, NOC::NOC_1})` (one_to_one:242).
maps.size_iter_params:
Page size = arch flit size: BLACKHOLE 64 bytes, WH 32 bytes (dm_common.cpp:21 obtain_page_size_bytes; loopback:209). bytes_per_transaction = pages_per_transaction * bytes_per_page.

one_to_one: l1_addr/test_id/dest_coords/num_vc are COMPILE-TIME args (named CTA map, one_to_one:93-97); num_of_transactions and bytes_per_transaction are RUNTIME args (\"num_tx\",\"tx_size\", one_to_one:108,134-137). The Metal-2.0 sender (sender_2_0.cpp) deliberately keeps num_tx/tx_size as runtime varargs, not CTAs, to avoid JIT cache reuse of a stale binary across the host sweep (comment at sender_2_0.cpp:18-22). Sweeps: packet_sizes_test num_of_transactions in {1,4,16,64,256} (*=4) x pages_per_transaction 1..max (*=2), capped 1024 pages (64KB) on BH (one_to_one:280-307). directed_ideal_test (ID 50): num_of_transactions=256, pages_per_transaction=max_transmittable_pages (one_to_one:203-204).

loopback: src_addr/dst_addr/page_size/test_id are COMPILE-TIME (loopback:80-84); num_of_transactions/transaction_num_pages/dest_x/dest_y are RUNTIME (loopback:100,131-134). Sweep: num_of_transactions {1,4,16,64,256} x transaction_size_pages 1..1024 (BH) (loopback:213-215). DirectedIdeal (ID 55): num_of_transactions=128, transaction_size_pages = max_transmittable_pages/(num_of_transactions*2) (divide by 2 because two buffers share L1) (loopback:243-245).

max_transmittable_pages comes from `compute_physical_constraints(mesh_device)` = {bytes_per_page, max_transmittable_bytes (= DEFAULT_UNRESERVED L1 size), max_transmittable_pages} (dm_common.cpp:47-58). Max total transaction == 64KB; max_pages_per_transaction on BH = 1024 (one_to_one:282, loopback:208).
maps.bw_measurement:
Measurement is on-device cycle counting via the Tracy profiler, post-processed in python. NOT measured on host.

IN-KERNEL: the timed region is `{ DeviceZoneScopedN(\"RISCV0\"); <write loop>; noc_async_write_barrier(); }` — the scope captures start/end device cycles INCLUDING the barrier (drain), so duration_cycles = cycles for all writes to fully complete. Each kernel also stamps attributes via `DeviceTimestampedData(\"Number of transactions\", N)`, `DeviceTimestampedData(\"Transaction size in bytes\", bytes)`, `DeviceTimestampedData(\"Test id\", id)`, and (one_to_one) `DeviceTimestampedData(\"NoC Index\", noc_index)` (sender.cpp:34-41). For one_to_one the stamped \"Number of transactions\" is the raw loop count; for all_to_all it is num_of_transactions*num_subordinates.

HOST/PYTHON: stats_collector.py parses the profiler CSV. Per series entry: `duration = entry[\"duration_cycles\"]`; effective bandwidth = `num_transactions * transaction_size / duration` in BYTES/CYCLE (stats_collector.py:165-170). Per-core override: if a kernel stamps `DeviceTimestampedData(\"Per-core bytes\", n)`, bandwidth_per_core = per_core_bytes/duration (stats_collector.py:172-179; documented at top README:154-159) — this is the hook for asymmetric all-core traffic. Aggregate across cores: median of per-core durations/bandwidths (stats_collector.py:199-203). COMBINED/aggregate multi-core bandwidth = total_bytes / wall_clock_time where wall_clock_time = max(end_cycle) - min(start_cycle) across all cores (i.e. first-core-start to last-core-end), falling back to max duration (stats_collector.py:207-225) — this is the aggregate NoC-bandwidth metric you want for an all-core kernel. Optional GB/s conversion: `bandwidth_gbps = agg_bandwidth(bytes/cyc) * clock_freq_ghz` using a \"Clock frequency MHz\" stamp if present (stats_collector.py:227-230). RISCV_PROCESSORS = [\"riscv_1\",\"riscv_0\"], riscv_0=Sender, riscv_1=Receiver (constants.py:6,30-31); for dual-NoC tests, NOC0 traffic shows on riscv_0 and NOC1 on riscv_1, filterable via heatmap -r riscv_0/riscv_1. NOC_WIDTHS (theoretical max bytes/cycle per NoC) = {wormhole_b0:32, blackhole:64} (constants.py:9-11); heatmap warns if a core exceeds it (heatmap.py:72-73). Pass/fail BW thresholds in test_bounds.yaml: \"One to One Directed Ideal\" blackhole riscv_0 = 59 B/cyc; \"Loopback Directed Ideal\" blackhole riscv_0 = 60 B/cyc; WH values 30/28 (test_bounds.yaml:19-27, 297-301).
key_apis: get_noc_addr(x, y, local_addr) -> uint64_t NoC address (kernel)
noc_async_write(src_local_addr, dst_noc_addr, size, noc_index, vc) and noc_async_write_barrier() (Gen1 dataflow_api.h)
Noc noc(noc_index); UnicastEndpoint; noc.async_write<NocOptions::CUSTOM_VC>(src_ep, dst_ep, size, src_traits, dst_traits, NocOptVals{.vc}); noc.async_write_barrier() (Gen2 endpoints.h)
noc_semaphore_inc(noc_addr,1) / Semaphore sem(sem::sem_name); sem.up(noc, x, y, 1) (completion signaling)
experimental KernelSpec / ProgramSpec / WorkUnitSpec / SemaphoreSpec / MakeProgramFromSpec / ProgramRunArgs / SetProgramRunArgs (Metal 2.0 host API)
gotchas: NoC routing DIRECTION (X-then-Y east/south vs Y-then-X north/west) is NOT chosen in the kernel — it is implicit per NoC index, selected on the host via DataMovementHardwareConfig::Gen1Config{.noc = NOC::NOC_0|NOC_1}. The reference tests only use one NoC per program (RISCV_0). For a dual-NoC all-core kernel you must launch BOTH a NOC0 (RISCV_0) and a NOC1 (RISCV_1) dataflow kernel, or use a dynamic-NoC path — neither one_to_one nor loopback demonstrates two NoCs in one program; one_to_one only sweeps NOC_0/NOC_1 in separate runs (one_to_one:242).

These reference senders write num_of_transactions copies to the SAME dst address (throughput loop), they do NOT scatter to distinct addresses. A real gather/scatter must vary src/dst addr per transaction.
BW is bytes/CYCLE by default (not GB/s); GB/s requires a 'Clock frequency MHz' DeviceTimestampedData stamp that these kernels do NOT emit. Add it if you want absolute GB/s.

The DeviceZoneScopedN region INCLUDES noc_async_write_barrier(), so duration measures completion-to-drain, not just issue latency. Place the barrier inside the zone (as the references do) or BW will be overstated.

Aggregate/combined multi-core bandwidth uses wall_clock_time = max(end_cycle)-min(start_cycle) across cores; this requires per-core start/end cycles to be recorded — only meaningful when all cores run the kernel (an all-core launch). The per-run 'Number of transactions'/'Transaction size' attributes are GLOBAL (same numerator on every core) unless a kernel stamps 'Per-core bytes' (stats_collector.py:172-179) — for asymmetric gather/scatter you MUST stamp 'Per-core bytes' per core or the heatmap/agg numbers will be wrong.
loopback needs L1 size >= 2*transaction_size (src and dst buffers on the same core at base and base+tx_size); directed-ideal halves max pages for this reason (loopback:66, 245).

Blackhole page/flit size is 64 bytes (vs 32 on WH) and max transaction is 64KB => max 1024 pages on BH (one_to_one:282, loopback:208-209). NOC_WIDTHS max = 64 B/cyc per NoC on Blackhole (constants.py:11); heatmap warns above this (heatmap.py:72).
Both tests run only on a single-device unit mesh via GenericMeshDeviceFixture and fast dispatch only (Mesh Device API does not support slow dispatch). Device coord is hardcoded MeshCoordinate({0,0}).
Coordinates are packed: one_to_one packs phys (x<<16)|(y&0xFFFF) into a single CTA and the kernel must unpack identically — keep the pack/unpack consistent if you reuse it.

To register a new all-core test you must add its test_*.cpp to sources.cmake (UNIT_TESTS_DATA_MOVEMENT_SRC, sources.cmake:4-40), assign a unique test_id (see README table / test_information.yaml), and add BW bounds to test_bounds.yaml keyed by test name + arch + riscv_0/riscv_1.

maps.component: tt-metal COMMON data_movement gtest framework (tests/tt_metal/tt_metal/data_movement) — fixtures, build/invocation, profiler wiring, and how to add an all-core dual-NoC gather/scatter test
maps.host_setup: DEVICE OPEN: All current DM tests use MeshDevice (not raw CreateDevice). They take `GenericMeshDeviceFixture` (multi_device_fixture.hpp:242), whose base `MeshDeviceFixtureBase::SetUp` (multi_device_fixture.hpp:116-167) skips if TT_METAL_SLOW_DISPATCH_MODE is set (fast-dispatch only) and calls `MeshDevice::create(MeshDeviceConfig(system_mesh_shape...), l1_small, trace_region, num_cqs=1, core_type, {}, worker_l1)`. Test bodies get the device via `auto mesh_device = get_mesh_device();` and then `IDevice* device = mesh_device->impl().get_device(0);` (one_to_one:47). Slow-dispatch tests instead use `MeshDeviceFixture` (device_fixture.hpp:18) which calls `distributed::MeshDevice::create_unit_meshes(...)` and requires TT_METAL_SLOW_DISPATCH_MODE. TearDown calls mesh_device_->close().\n\nPROGRAM/CORE SETUP — two coexisting styles. (A) Metal-2.0 ProgramSpec API (used by one_to_one/all_to_all 2_0 paths): build `KernelSpec` with `.source` (kernel path string), `.compile_time_args = KernelSpec::CompileTimeArgs{named map}`, `.runtime_arg_schema={.runtime_arg_names={...}}`, `.hw_config = DataMovementHardwareConfig{.gen1_config={.processor=DataMovementProcessor::RISCV_0, .noc=test_config.noc_id}, .gen2_config={}}`, optional `.advanced_options={.num_runtime_varargs=N}`; wrap in `ProgramSpec{.kernels={...}, .work_units={WorkUnitSpec{.kernels={id}, .target_nodes=core_set}}}`; `Program program = MakeProgramFromSpec(*mesh_device, spec);` then per-core RTAs via `ProgramRunArgs`/`SetProgramRunArgs(program, run_params)` (all_to_all:124-174). (B) Classic API (core_bidirectional.cpp:93): `CreateKernel(program, path, core, DataMovementConfig{.processor=RISCV_0, .noc=NOC::RISCV_0_default, .compile_args=...})`. CoreRange/CoreRangeSet describe the grid (`CoreRange(start,end)`, `corerange_to_cores`, `.merge`); full grid from `device->compute_with_storage_grid_size()`. L1 addr/size from `unit_tests::dm::get_l1_address_and_size(mesh_device, core)` and `compute_physical_constraints(mesh_device)` (dm_common.cpp). No explicit semaphores in these L1-to-L1 throughput tests — completion is via noc_async_write_barrier on-device + host Finish(); core_bidirectional shows how to put a writer on RISCV_0 and a reader on RISCV_1 on the SAME master core for simultaneous bidirectional traffic.
maps.kernel_code: Dataflow kernel is `void kernel_main()`. Classic style (all_to_all/kernels/sender.cpp): read CTAs via `get_compile_time_arg_val(i)` and per-core RTAs via `get_arg_val<uint32_t>(i)`; loop over subordinates building dst addr then loop num_of_transactions issuing `noc_async_write(src_l1_addr, dst_noc_addr, bytes, noc_index, vc)`; single `noc_async_write_barrier()` after all writes. Metal-2.0 style (sender_2_0.cpp): `get_arg(args::name)` for named CTAs, `get_arg(args::name)` for named runtime args, `get_vararg(idx++)` for the variadic per-subordinate (x,y) coordinate pairs; `Noc noc(noc_index); UnicastEndpoint unicast_endpoint;` then `noc.async_write<NocOptions::CUSTOM_VC>(unicast_endpoint, unicast_endpoint, bytes, {.addr=src}, {.noc_x, .noc_y, .addr=dst}, NocOptVals{.vc=...});` ending with `noc.async_write_barrier()`. For a 3-hop gather/scatter you would extend this loop to walk the partner list and issue reads (`noc_async_read`) for gather or writes for scatter; multicast would use the multicast write APIs (see one_to_all). The TIMED region is wrapped in `{ DeviceZoneScopedN("RISCV0"); ...transactions...; barrier; }` so the profiler captures only the NoC traffic. noc_index is the per-kernel NoC selected by the host hw_config/DataMovementConfig .noc field.
maps.partner_coord_logic: Logical->physical conversion is done HOST-SIDE: `device->worker_core_from_logical_core(logical_core)` returns the physical/worker CoreCoord. one_to_one packs a single partner into a CTA: `physical.x<<16 | (physical.y & 0xFFFF)` (test_one_to_one.cpp:87-89), unpacked in-kernel as `x=packed>>16, y=packed&0xFFFF`. all_to_all pushes ALL subordinate worker coords into a flat `vector<uint32_t>{x0,y0,x1,y1,...}` (test_all_to_all.cpp:95-100) delivered as runtime VARARGS per master core (`sender_run_params.advanced_options.runtime_varargs.emplace(mst_logical_core, sub_worker_coordinates)`), and the kernel rebuilds them with get_vararg pairs. The on-device NoC address is then `get_noc_addr(x, y, l1_local_addr)` (classic) or the struct `{.noc_x=x, .noc_y=y, .addr=...}` (2.0). NOTE: the framework computes partners on the host; for an all-core 3-hop X-then-Y (NOC0 east/south) vs Y-then-X (NOC1 north/west) scheme you'd compute each core's 3-hop partner host-side from logical coords, convert with worker_core_from_logical_core, and pass per-core via the varargs mechanism, choosing noc_id=NOC::NOC_0 vs NOC::NOC_1 in the KernelSpec hw_config to pick the routing NoC (the framework only sets which NoC the kernel uses; X-then-Y vs Y-then-X is the hardware's fixed per-NoC dimension-order routing, so NoC selection IS the route-direction selection).
maps.size_iter_params: bytes_per_page from `compute_physical_constraints` = 64 on BLACKHOLE, 32 otherwise (dm_common.cpp:21). bytes_per_transaction = pages_per_transaction * bytes_per_page. Sweeps: num_of_transactions and pages_per_transaction (one_to_one packet_sizes_test:284 loops num_tx 1..256 *=4, pages 1..max *=2; BLACKHOLE max_pages=1024 => 64KB max transaction). Directed-ideal uses few transactions x max pages (256 tx of max_transmittable_pages). PASSING: test_id, l1 addr, packed/flat partner coords, and num_vc are COMPILE-TIME args (named CTA map for 2.0, vector<uint32_t> compile_args for classic). num_of_transactions and bytes_per_transaction (tx_size) are RUNTIME args — deliberately runtime (not CTA) so the JIT cache does not reuse a stale binary across sweep iterations (comment at sender_2_0.cpp:18-19). all_to_all passes per-subordinate coordinate pairs as runtime VARARGS (advanced_options.num_runtime_varargs = num_subordinates*2).
maps.bw_measurement: Cycle timing is captured ON-DEVICE by the Tracy/kernel profiler. The kernel wraps its NoC loop in `DeviceZoneScopedN("RISCV0")` (a paired ZONE_START/ZONE_END timestamp) and emits per-run scalars with `DeviceTimestampedData(\"name\", value)` — every DM kernel stamps \"Test id\", \"NoC Index\", \"Number of transactions\", \"Transaction size in bytes\" (and optional \"Per-core bytes\" for asymmetric/multicast tests; README:154-159). HOST measurement is post-process only: the C++ binary writes the device profiler CSV; the Python harness drives it. test_data_movement.py:89 runs `TT_METAL_DEVICE_PROFILER=1 TT_METAL_PROFILER_PROGRAM_SUPPORT_COUNT=1333 $TT_METAL_HOME/build/test/tt_metal/unit_tests_data_movement [--gtest_filter=...]`, then StatsCollector (stats_collector.py) parses the CSV via tracy `import_log_run_stats` using an \"adjacent\" ZONE_START->ZONE_END analysis per RISC (riscv_0=BRISC, riscv_1=NCRISC). Bandwidth per run = `Number of transactions * Transaction size / duration_cycles` (bytes/cycle); aggregated by median across cores (aggregate_performance, line 170,199). Combined/aggregate multi-core BW = total_bytes / wall_clock_time where wall_clock_time = max(end_cycle)-min(start_cycle) across all cores (lines 207-225) — THIS is the metric to use for aggregate all-core dual-NoC bandwidth. Optional GB/s if \"Clock frequency MHz\" stamped (line 228-230). PerformanceChecker compares against test_bounds.yaml.
key_apis: Metal2.0: KernelSpec / ProgramSpec / WorkUnitSpec / MakeProgramFromSpec / ProgramRunArgs / SetProgramRunArgs
kernel: noc_async_write / noc_async_read / noc_async_write_barrier; Noc.async_write<CUSTOM_VC>(...); get_noc_addr(x,y,addr)
gotchas: ADD-A-TEST checklist (README:98-123 + sources.cmake): (1) make a new dir e.g. data_movement/gather_scatter_3hop/ with test_*.cpp + kernels/; (2) pick an unused test_id (next free integer — 316+ is open after all_to_all 300-315; matmul uses 1000-1231, noc_estimator 800-817); (3) register name in python/test_mappings/test_information.yaml and optional perf bounds in test_bounds.yaml (keyed by test NAME string -> arch wormhole_b0/blackhole -> riscv_0/riscv_1 -> bandwidth) and per-type attrs in test_type_attributes.yaml; (4) add the .cpp path to the UNIT_TESTS_DATA_MOVEMENT_SRC list in sources.cmake (NOT CMakeLists.txt directly — CMakeLists just includes sources.cmake); (5) add a README.md in the dir; (6) rebuild with ./build_metal.sh --build-tests.

BUILD: `./build_metal.sh --build-tests` produces binary `build/test/tt_metal/unit_tests_data_movement` (CMakeLists RUNTIME_OUTPUT_DIRECTORY = build/test/tt_metal). UNITY build is ON (TT_ENABLE_UNITY_BUILD) — keep file-local symbols in anonymous namespaces / unique names to avoid ODR clashes across the unity blob.

RUN: direct gtest `./build/test/tt_metal/unit_tests_data_movement --gtest_filter="*YourTestName*"`. For bandwidth numbers run via pytest: `pytest tests/tt_metal/tt_metal/data_movement/python/test_data_movement.py --gtest-filter=... --plot --report --arch blackhole`; it sets TT_METAL_DEVICE_PROFILER=1 and TT_METAL_PROFILER_PROGRAM_SUPPORT_COUNT=1333 itself. Profiling REQUIRES a profiler-enabled build (build_metal with profiler) or the CSV won't exist.

DISPATCH MODE: GenericMeshDeviceFixture is FAST-DISPATCH ONLY and GTEST_SKIPs if TT_METAL_SLOW_DISPATCH_MODE is set. Do NOT set that env var for a normal DM test. Slow-dispatch-only suites use MeshDeviceFixture instead.

NoC SELECTION = ROUTE DIRECTION: the framework lets each kernel pick NOC::NOC_0 vs NOC::NOC_1 via hw_config.gen1_config.noc (or DataMovementConfig.noc). The X-then-Y (east/south) vs Y-then-X (north/west) behavior is the hardware's fixed per-NoC dimension-order routing — you select it purely by which NoC the kernel runs on; there is no per-transaction route-order flag in these APIs. To exercise BOTH NoCs simultaneously per core, place two kernels on one core (RISCV_0+NOC_0 and RISCV_1+NOC_1) as core_bidirectional does (test_core_bidirectional.cpp:93-145).

PROFILER COUNTER LIMIT: TT_METAL_PROFILER_PROGRAM_SUPPORT_COUNT=1333 caps how many distinct profiled program launches are retained; large all-core sweeps (many run_host_ids) can exceed it and silently drop runs — keep the sweep bounded or raise the count.

BW NUMERATOR ASYMMETRY: default per-run bandwidth = Number_of_transactions * Transaction_size / duration assumes uniform per-core traffic. For gather/scatter where senders and receivers move different byte counts, stamp `DeviceTimestampedData("Per-core bytes", actual_bytes)` per core so stats_collector sums real bytes for the combined/aggregate metric instead of over/under-counting (stats_collector.py:174-225, README:154-159).

runtime_host_id is a global counter (dm_common.cpp:18) incremented per run via program.set_runtime_id; each profiled config must call it or runs collide in the CSV. num_of_transactions/tx_size must stay RUNTIME args (not CTAs) or the JIT binary cache reuses a stale kernel across sweep iterations (sender_2_0.cpp:18-19).

maps.component: Blackhole dual-NoC dataflow API: exact signatures/semantics of get_noc_addr, noc_async_read/write, barriers, multicast, get_noc_multicast_addr, NOC0/NOC1 selection, plus host harness + bandwidth-measurement reference patterns and Blackhole-specific NoC flush/ND-mismatch warnings.
maps.host_setup: Device: tt_metal::distributed::MeshDevice::create_unit_mesh(device_id) (test_noc_rtor.cpp:133); grid via device->compute_with_storage_grid_size() (:136). Program: tt_metal::Program() / CreateProgram. CoreRange: CoreRange all_cores({0,0},{cx-1,cy-1}) covers all worker cores (test_noc_rtor.cpp:158-160); CoreRangeSet for non-rectangular. CB: tt_metal::CircularBufferConfig(num_tiles*tile_size,{{cb_index,DataFormat::Float16_b}}).set_page_size(cb_index,tile_size) then CreateCircularBuffer(program,core,cfg) (test_noc_rtor.cpp:167-170) — provides L1 src/dst backing buffer per core. Kernel creation selecting NoC: tt_metal::CreateKernel(program, path, cores, tt_metal::DataMovementConfig{.processor = (noc==0)?RISCV_0:RISCV_1, .noc = (noc==0)?NOC::RISCV_0_default:NOC::RISCV_1_default, .noc_mode = DM_DEDICATED_NOC or DM_DYNAMIC_NOC}) (test_noc_rtor.cpp:174-184; kernel_types.hpp:33-43,63-66). For BOTH NoCs on the same core simultaneously you launch TWO data-movement kernels: BRISC/RISCV_0 with .noc=NOC_0 and NCRISC/RISCV_1 with .noc=NOC_1 (test_dynamic_noc.cpp:75-92), OR set .noc_mode=DM_DYNAMIC_NOC and address the other NoC in-kernel via 1-noc_index. enum NOC{RISCV_0_default=0,RISCV_1_default=1,NOC_0=0,NOC_1=1}; enum NOC_MODE{DM_DEDICATED_NOC=0,DM_DYNAMIC_NOC=1} (kernel_types.hpp:33-43). Semaphore: tt_metal::CreateSemaphore(program, core_range_set, init_val[, CoreType]) returns a uint32_t id (test_dynamic_noc.cpp:66-69); in-kernel resolve with get_semaphore(id). Runtime args: tt_metal::SetRuntimeArgs(program, kernel, core, std::array/vector) (test_noc_rtor.cpp:193, test_dynamic_noc.cpp:143). Launch+time: build MeshWorkload, EnqueueMeshWorkload(cq, wl, false) then Finish(cq) (test_noc_rtor.cpp:201-207).
maps.kernel_code: kernel_main() reads compile-time args via get_compile_time_arg_val(i) (e.g. iteration, page_size, num_riscs — dynamic_noc_writer.cpp:12-14) and runtime args via get_arg_val<uint32_t>(i) (partner noc_x/noc_y, mcast corners, num_dests, risc_index, sem ids — :16-29). Local L1 src/dst pointer from CB: get_read_ptr(cb_id)/get_write_ptr(cb_id) (dynamic_noc_writer.cpp:32; noc_read.cpp:21). Per transfer: build remote addr with get_noc_addr(noc_x,noc_y,l1_addr,noc) then noc_async_read(src_noc_addr,dst_l1,size,noc) / noc_async_write(src_l1,dst_noc_addr,size,noc); after issuing N async ops call noc_async_read_barrier(noc)/noc_async_write_barrier(noc) to wait for completion (noc_read.cpp:25-27). Multicast: noc_async_write_multicast(src_l1, get_noc_multicast_addr(x0,y0,x1,y1,l1_addr,noc), size, num_dests, linked=false, noc) — num_dests excludes self; use noc_async_write_multicast_loopback_src if sender must also receive (dynamic_noc_writer.cpp:114-127). Dual-NoC in one kernel: issue on self NoC with `noc_index` and on the other NoC with `1 - noc_index` in the same loop (dynamic_noc_writer.cpp:48-49,82-90). Loop structure for a 3-hop gather/scatter: per hop compute partner addr -> issue noc_async_read (gather) or noc_async_write (scatter) on the chosen NoC -> optionally pipeline several before a single barrier (issuing barrier every iteration as noc_read.cpp:27 does serializes and kills bandwidth — see gotchas). Final: barrier on BOTH NoCs: for(noc=0;noc<NUM_NOCS;noc++) noc_async_full_barrier(noc) or per-direction read/write barriers (dynamic_noc_writer.cpp:142-144,192-199). Cross-RISC sync via noc_semaphore_inc/noc_semaphore_wait (:147-160). EXACT signatures: template<uint32_t max_page_size=NOC_MAX_BURST_SIZE+1,bool enable_noc_tracing=true> inline void noc_async_read(uint64_t src_noc_addr, uint32_t dst_local_l1_addr, uint32_t size, uint8_t noc=noc_index, uint32_t read_req_vc=NOC_UNICAST_WRITE_VC); template<uint32_t max_page_size=NOC_MAX_BURST_SIZE+1,bool enable_noc_tracing=true,bool posted=false> inline void noc_async_write(uint32_t src_local_l1_addr, uint64_t dst_noc_addr, uint32_t size, uint8_t noc=noc_index, uint32_t vc=NOC_UNICAST_WRITE_VC); template<uint32_t max_page_size=NOC_MAX_BURST_SIZE+1> inline void noc_async_write_multicast(uint32_t src_local_l1_addr, uint64_t dst_noc_addr_multicast, uint32_t size, uint32_t num_dests, bool linked=false, uint8_t noc=noc_index, uint8_t vc=NOC_MULTICAST_WRITE_VC); void noc_async_read_barrier(uint8_t noc=noc_index); FORCE_INLINE void noc_async_write_barrier(uint8_t noc=noc_index); FORCE_INLINE void noc_async_writes_flushed(uint8_t noc=noc_index); FORCE_INLINE void noc_async_full_barrier(uint8_t noc_idx=noc_index).
maps.partner_coord_logic: Host: convert logical core to physical NoC-0 coords with device->worker_core_from_logical_core(CoreCoord) (test_dynamic_noc.cpp:121,125,127), then pass physical .x/.y into the kernel as runtime args (:128-137). Device->worker_core_from_logical_core returns NOC-0 ('physical') coordinates. Kernel: build the 64-bit NoC address with get_noc_addr(noc_x, noc_y, l1_addr, noc) -> returns NOC_XY_ADDR(DYNAMIC_NOC_X(noc,x),DYNAMIC_NOC_Y(noc,y),addr) (dataflow_api_addrgen.h:212-215). NOC_XY_ADDR packs y<<(36+6) | x<<36 | addr (noc_parameters.h:336-338; NOC_ADDR_LOCAL_BITS=36, NOC_ADDR_NODE_ID_BITS=6, coord shift 36). CRITICAL Blackhole nuance: DYNAMIC_NOC_X/Y resolve to NOC_0_X/NOC_0_Y which are IDENTITY on BH (`#define NOC_0_X(noc_index,size,x) x`, noc_nonblocking_api.h:44-45). So get_noc_addr/get_noc_multicast_addr do NOT auto-mirror coordinates for NOC1 — you pass the SAME physical NoC-0 coordinates for both NoCs; the hardware routes NOC0 X-then-Y (east/south) and NOC1 Y-then-X (north/west) internally. The coordinate-mirroring macros NOC_0_X_PHYS_COORD/NOC_0_Y_PHYS_COORD (= noc_index==0 ? c : size-1-c, noc_nonblocking_api.h:46-47) exist but are NOT used by get_noc_addr. For multicast: get_noc_multicast_addr(x_start,y_start,x_end,y_end,addr,noc) (dataflow_api_addrgen.h:182-196) — the rectangle's start/end corners must be ordered to match the NoC's traversal direction: for NOC0 use (top_left -> bottom_right); for NOC1 swap to (bottom_right -> top_left). dynamic_noc_writer.cpp:36-46 shows exactly this corner swap keyed on noc_index. To address a partner on the opposite NoC from inside the kernel, pass noc = 1 - noc_index (dynamic_noc_writer.cpp:48-49,83-86). For DRAM/L1 interleaved partners use TensorAccessor + get_noc_addr(id, accessor[, offset, noc]) (dataflow_api_addrgen.h:303-407).
maps.size_iter_params: transfer size (bytes) and iteration/hop count are typically COMPILE-TIME args: get_compile_time_arg_val(0)=iteration, (1)=page_size, (2)=num_riscs (dynamic_noc_writer.cpp:12-14), passed via DataMovementConfig.compile_args vector. Variable per-core data (partner coords, mcast corners, num_dests, semaphore ids, buffer base addr) are RUNTIME args via SetRuntimeArgs + get_arg_val<uint32_t>(i) (dynamic_noc_writer.cpp:16-29; test_dynamic_noc.cpp:128-145; test_noc_rtor.cpp passes core_index,l1_buffer_addr,num_tiles,total_cores at :192). page_size in the rtor example = single_tile_size = 2*1024 = 2KB (test_noc_rtor.cpp:140-141); size argument to noc_async_read/write is per-transaction bytes (valid 0..1MB per docstring). NOC_MAX_BURST_SIZE on Blackhole = NOC_MAX_BURST_WORDS(256) * NOC_WORD_BYTES(NOC_PAYLOAD_WIDTH 512 / 8 = 64) = 16384 bytes (noc_parameters.h:287-290); transfers <= this take the one-packet fast path (max_page_size template <= NOC_MAX_BURST_SIZE selects noc_async_*_one_packet, dataflow_api.h:566,838), larger use *_any_len. Aggregate bytes for bandwidth = size * iterations * hops * num_active_cores * num_nocs.
maps.bw_measurement: Two methods in the reference harness. (1) Host wall-clock: wrap EnqueueMeshWorkload(cq,wl,false)+Finish(cq) in std::chrono::steady_clock::now() before/after, take duration_cast<microseconds> (test_noc_rtor.cpp:204-209); aggregate BW = total_bytes_moved / elapsed_seconds (includes dispatch overhead, so amortize with large iteration counts and average over num_tests via calculate_average, :229). (2) Device profiler cycles (more accurate, excludes host overhead): build with profiler enabled, then get_t0_to_any_riscfw_end_cycle(device, program) returns kernel cycle span (test_noc_rtor.cpp:214; util.hpp:22 — returns 0 unless rtoptions().get_profiler_enabled()), convert to time with clock_freq_mhz from get_tt_npu_clock(device) (test_noc_rtor.cpp:135,216). For an all-core run sum bytes across every participating core and both NoCs, divide by max(end_cycle)/clock to get aggregate GB/s. Counting note: kernels can also self-timestamp with the device profiler markers around the issue loop. Build/run requires profiler build (TT_METAL profiler) for method (2).

gotchas: BLACKHOLE ND-mismatch/flush warning (BlackholeBringUpProgrammingGuide.md:95): on BH, NoC commands issued without explicit flushes caused ND (non-deterministic) mismatches or hangs because data/semaphore signals were updated faster than the NoC could service the command. BH RISC-to-L1 latency is now lower than NoC latency (unlike WH), so you MUST add explicit flushes/barriers (noc_async_writes_flushed / noc_async_write_barrier) before signaling completion or reusing buffers. This is the single most important correctness constraint for a multi-hop gather/scatter kernel.

L1 write-through data cache on BH (4x16B cachelines): a reader on another core that previously read an address must invalidate before re-reading; the barriers internally call invalidate_l1_cache() (dataflow_api.h:1743,1748,1778,1860). Cache is disabled by default; enable via TT_METAL_ENABLE_L1_DATA_CACHE_RISCVS or set_l1_data_cache<true>() (must pair with set_l1_data_cache<false>() before kernel exit). Missed invalidations show up as ND data corruption.

Coordinates are NOT auto-mirrored for NOC1 on BH: DYNAMIC_NOC_X/Y == NOC_0_X/Y are identity macros (noc_nonblocking_api.h:44-45), so get_noc_addr returns the same packed address regardless of noc arg. Pass physical NoC-0 coordinates for both NoCs; routing direction (NOC0 X-then-Y east/south vs NOC1 Y-then-X north/west) is a hardware property of the chosen NoC, not something the address encodes. Do NOT try to manually flip unicast coords for NOC1.

Multicast rectangle corner ordering DOES matter per-NoC: for NOC0 use (top_left -> bottom_right), for NOC1 swap to (bottom_right -> top_left) so the start corner is the first one the NoC reaches (dynamic_noc_writer.cpp:36-46). Wrong ordering yields empty/incorrect mcast coverage.

noc_async_write_multicast num_dests EXCLUDES self: when mcasting to an NxN grid that includes the sender, pass num_dests = grid_count - 1; data is NOT written to self. Use noc_async_write_multicast_loopback_src (num_dests = full count) if the sender must also receive (dataflow_api.h:904-916; dynamic_noc_writer.cpp:111,125).

Per-iteration barrier kills bandwidth: the reference noc_read.cpp:26-27 calls noc_async_read_barrier() every iteration which serializes each transaction. For an aggregate-bandwidth measurement, issue many async reads/writes (pipeline across both NoCs and across hops) and barrier only once at the end, otherwise you measure latency-bound, not BW-bound, throughput.

DM_DEDICATED_NOC vs DM_DYNAMIC_NOC: in dedicated mode each RISC owns one fixed NoC; to drive BOTH NoCs you either run two kernels (BRISC->NOC0, NCRISC->NOC1, test_dynamic_noc.cpp:75-92) or set .noc_mode=DM_DYNAMIC_NOC and pass noc / 1-noc_index per call. noc_mode changes the barrier implementation (ncrisc_dynamic_* vs ncrisc_noc_* paths, dataflow_api.h:1741-1747,1876+). Must match host config and in-kernel usage.

Default VCs differ DRAM-vs-Tensix build: NOC_UNICAST_WRITE_VC and NOC_MULTICAST_WRITE_VC have two definitions (2/8 vs 1/4) selected by build context (dataflow_api_common.h:57-63); read VC reuses NOC_UNICAST_WRITE_VC as the default read_req_vc arg — be deliberate if mixing read/write VCs to avoid head-of-line blocking when measuring contention.
noc_inline_dw_write has a known HW issue on BH and is guarded out (#ifndef ARCH_BLACKHOLE, dynamic_noc_writer.cpp:135-138) — do not rely on inline dword writes for BH semaphore signaling.

NOC_MAX_BURST_SIZE on BH = 16384 bytes (256 words * 64B). Transfers <= this hit the single-packet fast path; size>16KB uses *_any_len which splits into multiple packets. Pick transfer size deliberately relative to 16KB when reporting per-transaction BW.
noc_nonblocking_api.h for BH carries a TODO comment (line 51) noting it is currently a WH-B0 copy not yet reviewed for BH; treat low-level cmd-buf assumptions with caution.
